  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "This is a false positive for CWE-094. The dangerous sink (`GroovyUtils.evalScriptByName`) exists, but the report misidentifies the source: these callsites do not execute user-supplied Groovy code. They only pass a `scriptName`, and the evaluator resolves that name to an already-registered `GroovyScript` or built-in script, then executes the stored `script.getContent()`. Those script contents are managed through administrator-only settings, so normal issue/build/UI users can at most trigger execution of trusted admin-configured code. Even if a request tampers with `scriptName`, an unknown name is rejected, and an existing name still does not give the attacker control over the code being executed. The `isAuthorized()` behavior cited in the report may be relevant to a separate authorization/design issue, but it does not make these callsites a real CWE-094 code-injection vulnerability.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "Reported callsites such as `server-core/src/main/java/io/onedev/server/model/support/issue/field/instance/ScriptingValue.java:63-66` do `Object result = GroovyUtils.evalScriptByName(scriptName, variables);`. In `server-core/src/main/java/io/onedev/server/util/GroovyUtils.java:68-97`, `evalScriptByName` does not evaluate `scriptName` as code; it looks up an existing script by exact name from `SettingManager.getGroovyScripts()` or built-in `ScriptContribution`s, and only then runs `evalScript(StringUtils.join(script.getContent(), \"\\n\"), variables)`. If the name is not found it throws `ExplicitException(\"Groovy script not found: \" + scriptName)`. Script creation/modification is administrator-only: `server-core/src/main/java/io/onedev/server/rest/resource/SettingResource.java:101-104` and `255-257` both require `SecurityUtils.isAdministrator()` for `/settings/groovy-scripts`, and the admin UI inherits `server-core/src/main/java/io/onedev/server/web/page/admin/AdministrationPage.java:17-20`, which also requires administrator permission. The selection UI enumerates existing script names instead of raw Groovy text (`server-core/src/main/java/io/onedev/server/web/editable/scriptchoice/ScriptChoiceEditor.java:42-47`). Project-side configuration pages also require management rights (`server-core/src/main/java/io/onedev/server/web/page/project/setting/ProjectSettingPage.java:20-22`). Therefore the reachable behavior is execution of preconfigured trusted scripts by design, not attacker-controlled code injection.",

  "report": "5
## Vulnerability 5: Script-Driven Dynamic Input Providers (24+ callsites)
Multiple `ScriptingDefaultValue`, `ScriptingChoices`, and `ScriptingValue` classes across the codebase call `GroovyUtils.evalScriptByName()` to dynamically compute field defaults and dropdown options:
| File | Context |
|---|---|
| `buildspecmodel/inputspec/textinput/defaultvalueprovider/ScriptingDefaultValue.java` | Build input defaults |
| `buildspecmodel/inputspec/choiceinput/choiceprovider/ScriptingChoices.java` | Build choice fields |
| `model/support/issue/field/instance/ScriptingValue.java:66` | Issue field values |
| `buildspec/param/instance/ScriptingValue.java:63` | Build parameter values |
| ...and 20+ more | |
Example from `model/support/issue/field/instance/ScriptingValue.java`:
```java
// Lines 63-66
Map<String, Object> variables = new HashMap<>();
variables.put("build", Build.get());
Object result = GroovyUtils.evalScriptByName(scriptName, variables);
```
These are triggered by issue/build UI interactions, meaning script execution happens in response to user-facing requests, not just admin actions. Authorization is delegated to `isAuthorized()` which has the bypass described in Vulnerability 2."
